Prepared for Sage Mountain Advisors • July 10, 2026

July 10th Debrief

Nine buildings toured across Buckhead & Central Perimeter — seven advancing to shortlist rankings
Tour Summary Rankings Next Steps
Section 01

This Week's Tours

Nine buildings toured over two days, presented in tour order. Each card covers the fundamentals, the defining strengths and trade-offs from the walkthrough, and — expanded below each card — the full notes including how each trade-off can be addressed.

First Stop
Tuesday Tour

Glenridge Highlands I

View building details →
What Stood Out
  • Best location from an average commute and access standpoint
  • Tasteful lobbies and a lot of the renovations
  • Great energy
  • Right-sized space on the 3rd floor with high TI potential
Trade-Offs
  • 3rd floor lacks natural light due to trees, the parking deck, and walls with 33% less glass
  • Lacking views
How we can address trade-offs
  • Landlord will contribute a higher TI allowance in this space to make up for the lower level and tired condition of the current build-out.

Second Stop
Tuesday Tour

Glenridge Highlands II

View building details →
What Stood Out
  • Best location from an average commute and access standpoint
  • Tasteful lobbies and a lot of the renovations
  • Great energy
  • Strong natural light
  • Efficient layout
Trade-Offs
  • Full floors only — corridors would need to be built if not taking the full 25,000 SF, which could hurt TI expenditure from the landlord
How we can address trade-offs
  • Find a floor where more of the existing improvements can be reused to lower TI spend.

Third Stop
Tuesday Tour

King

View building details →
Not advanced to shortlist rankings
What Stood Out
  • Impressive landscape and grounds upon entering
  • True Class A lobby
  • Convenient location
  • Large building cafés
  • Right-sized space on the 26th floor
  • Lifetime directly onsite
Trade-Offs
  • Aside from the landscape, grounds, and lobby, the building felt old and tired (hallways, bathrooms, windows)
  • Natural light limited by seemingly tinted windows
  • A sense of inefficiency in the floorplans — expansive elevator bank, angular glass, and large columns throughout
  • Large detached parking deck system to navigate
  • Higher price
How we can address trade-offs
  • Creative architectural design to deal with inefficiencies and maximize natural light; strong negotiations with the landlord to upgrade common hallways/bathrooms plus TI for the space itself.

Fourth Stop
Tuesday Tour

Summit Two

View building details →
What Stood Out
  • True Class A vibes
  • Great amenities
  • Strong natural light
  • Great views
  • Awesome event space
Trade-Offs
  • Less convenient location than the alternatives
  • Nice restaurants nearby but no true building café
  • Would need to split up a full floor
How we can address trade-offs
  • Approach under discussion.

Salesforce Tower

View building details →
Not advanced to shortlist rankings
What Stood Out
  • Nice lobby
  • Familiar location
  • Nice amenities
  • Fitting space with the Rubicon Suite
  • Nice views
  • Steam room in the fitness center
Trade-Offs
  • Similar same-old feeling as Resurgens — with Resurgens also in view
  • Still dealing with paid parking
  • Low-occupancy vibes
How we can address trade-offs
  • Negotiate parking and a high TI package.

Second Stop
Thursday Tour

3630 Peachtree

View building details →
What Stood Out
  • Best natural light of the set
  • Highest quality building
  • Feels like a great experience when clients visit
  • Nice restaurants onsite (Tomo and F&B)
Trade-Offs
  • Most expensive building by a significant amount
  • More expensive parking
How we can address trade-offs
  • Get more efficient with the layout (smaller offices, utilize building conference facilities, etc.) and negotiate parking.

Third Stop
Thursday Tour

Palisades — Building D

View building details →
What Stood Out
  • Two private balconies comparable in size to One Premier
  • Convenient location
  • Significantly lower rental rate
  • Simple building with the necessities
  • Good energy across the campus
  • Nice outdoor area with good shade and a water feature
Trade-Offs
  • Slightly lesser overall building quality
  • Small but existent window piers that could block some natural light
How we can address trade-offs
  • Take advantage of the lower rate: lease more space to allow for expansion space or unique features (i.e., a larger speakeasy), make the space itself more high-end, and control the experience separate from the building.

Fourth Stop
Thursday Tour

One Premier Plaza

View building details →
What Stood Out
  • Awesome balconies with a great view
  • Full floor at the right size
  • Tall ceilings
  • Good natural light
  • Great location
  • Simplicity
  • Lower rental rate
Trade-Offs
  • Worst fitness center of the set
  • Low on EV charging stations
How we can address trade-offs
  • Ownership is upgrading the bathrooms/locker areas and exploring whether the gym can be expanded; strong nearby gym options (two tiers of Lifetime, plus an LA Fitness off Abernathy). Rangewater added an additional EV outlet at the bottom of the parking deck — potential to negotiate another charger, though it may eat into the total TI allowance.

Resurgens Plaza

View building details →
What Stood Out
  • Familiar
  • Still feels like a quality option compared to others
  • Can reuse most of the existing build-out
  • Great views
  • Sized accordingly
  • Hungry landlord
Trade-Offs
  • Parking cost is still a factor
  • Significant window piers
  • Same-old feeling
How we can address trade-offs
  • Negotiate a large TI package and reduced parking.

Full Floor figures are an illustrative reference — Sage Mountain's requirement is ~20,000–25,000 SF, so most options wouldn't take an entire floor. The Apples-to-Apples row applies each building's quoted rate to a common 20,000 SF for a like-for-like cost comparison. All figures use the quoted rate before concessions.

Weighted Rankings

Each building earns one overall score built from its attribute rankings and weighted by Sage Mountain's stated priority order. Every attribute is worth points equal to its priority rank: the top priority (Commute) counts ×8, down to the lowest (Signage) at ×1. A building's 1–7 ranking on each attribute is multiplied by that weight, and the eight results are added together — so the highest possible score is 252 (a perfect 7 on all eight attributes).

The weighting scale — highest priority to lowest
Priority #1×8Commute
Priority #2×7Natural Light
Priority #3×6Parking Rate
Priority #4×5Food Options
Priority #5×4Private Balcony
Priority #6×3Fitness Center
Priority #7×2Construction & Design Quality
Priority #8×1Signage Opportunity
Important These rankings weigh Sage Mountain's eight priority attributes only — rental rate and cost are not factored into the score. Cost is shown separately in the Rental Rate, Full Floor Size, and Full Floor Cost rows above.

How to read this: the top three are closely grouped. Glenridge Highlands II edges ahead because its strengths land in the highest-priority attributes (commute, parking). Where a feature isn't offered (e.g. a private balcony), it scores zero on that attribute. Note that Palisades (#2) carries the lowest quoted rate of the set — but rate is not part of this score.
Section 03

Next Steps

The path from here to test-fits. Dates are rough estimates and sequence off the workplace strategy engagement.

1
Mid-July 2026
Team Interviews
MurphyMeyers to select meeting times for team interviews.
2
Est. August 21, 2026
Workplace Strategy
Estimated six weeks from the first meeting.
3
Est. Late August 2026
Second-Round Tours
Tour buildings with Scott and Tony immediately after strategy results. Ideally after the workplace strategy is complete, but touring in advance is an option if desired.
4
Est. Early September 2026
Shortlist & RFPs
Shortlist viable options and submit proposals/RFPs immediately after the second round of tours and strategy results — all sent at the same time to keep each option competing in lockstep.
5
Est. Early–Mid September 2026
Architectural Test-Fits
Roughly one week after receiving proposal responses from the landlords. Can begin earlier if desired and bill the landlord later.
Industry Real Estate Partners
Prepared by Matt Parisi • Vice President • July 2026
